stem:
A 52-year-old man presents to your emergency department with three weeks of fever, anorexia and a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. He had a bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement nine months ago for severe aortic regurgitation. On examination he is febrile, has a soft early diastolic murmur unchanged from baseline, splinter haemorrhages on two fingers and tender nodules on his left finger pad.

Two sets of blood cultures drawn at separate venepuncture sites 12 hours apart have grown Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis biotype I). Transthoracic echo shows no clear vegetation but limited views.

data_table.Examination: Early diastolic murmur unchanged; Janeway lesions, Osler nodes; no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Two sets blood cultures positive Streptococcus gallolyticus; CRP 96; WCC 13.2; TTE limited
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg daily; perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Transoesophageal echocardiography to evaluate prosthetic valve and assess for vegetations, abscess, or new regurgitation now
options.rationale: TTE has low sensitivity for prosthetic valve endocarditis and abscess; TOE is the test of choice when there is high pretest probability with negative or non-diagnostic TTE in a prosthetic valve patient. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis 2024)

options.text: Cardiac CT angiography with delayed-phase imaging as a first-line replacement for echocardiography to detect vegetations and abscess formation
options.rationale: Cardiac CT has a role as an adjunct, particularly for prosthetic valves and pseudoaneurysm assessment, but TOE remains the first-line imaging after non-diagnostic TTE in suspected prosthetic endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis 2024)

options.text: Cardiac MRI as a first-line imaging modality to evaluate vegetations and associated annular or myocardial extension at presentation
options.rationale: Cardiac MRI is not first-line in suspected endocarditis; its role is limited compared with TOE for vegetation and abscess detection on prosthetic valves. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis 2024)

options.text: Repeat transthoracic echocardiography in 48 hours with intravenous contrast to improve windows and revisit vegetation question
options.rationale: Repeat TTE adds limited yield over the initial TTE in a prosthetic valve case and delays diagnosis; TOE is significantly more sensitive and is the appropriate next step. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis 2024)

options.text: PET-CT with FDG to localise an occult source of bacteraemia before considering further cardiac imaging in this prosthetic valve patient today
options.rationale: FDG-PET is an adjunct supporting major Duke criteria in prosthetic valve endocarditis after at least three months, not the first imaging step in this acute presentation with persistent bacteraemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Endocarditis 2024)

explanation.summary: Modified Duke criteria require positive blood cultures with typical organisms plus evidence of endocardial involvement. Streptococcus gallolyticus is a typical organism. TTE has limited sensitivity for prosthetic valve endocarditis (around 50%) so TOE is the test of choice when pretest probability is high. Additionally, S. gallolyticus bacteraemia warrants colonoscopy because of its association with colorectal neoplasia. Empirical therapy in prosthetic valve endocarditis is broader than native valve and includes vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin pending sensitivities.
key_points: TTE sensitivity for prosthetic valve endocarditis is around 50%
TOE is first-line imaging when pretest probability is high
S. gallolyticus is a typical Duke organism and warrants colonoscopy
Empirical prosthetic valve cover includes vancomycin, gentamicin and rifampicin
explanation.pearls: When prosthetic valve endocarditis is clinically likely and TTE is non-diagnostic, proceed straight to TOE within 24 to 48 hours and arrange colonoscopy if blood cultures grow S. gallolyticus rather than chasing CT or PET first.

stem: A 44-year-old woman presents to your general practice clinic with new-onset hypertension (BP 162/96 today), 9 kg weight gain over six months, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness climbing stairs, and oligomenorrhoea. On examination she has central adiposity, supraclavicular fat pads, violaceous abdominal striae greater than 1 cm wide, and thin skin. She drinks two standard drinks weekly and takes no glucocorticoids including inhaled, intra-articular, or topical products. She is otherwise well. You suspect endogenous Cushing's syndrome and need to choose an initial screening investigation.

data_table.Examination: BMI 33; supraclavicular fat pads; wide violaceous striae; proximal weakness
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 8.4 mmol/L (random); K 3.4; HbA1c 7.1%; normal TSH

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial screening test?

options.text: Random morning serum cortisol with simultaneous ACTH measurement on a single clinic visit for initial screening at first presentation
options.rationale: Random cortisol has too much diurnal and stress variability to be used as a screening test for Cushing's syndrome and is not recommended at initial workup. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Syndrome Guideline 2008)

options.text: Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test with 8 am cortisol on the following morning after the patient takes 1 mg at bedtime
options.rationale: The 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test is one of three recommended first-line screens (alongside 24-hour urinary free cortisol and late-night salivary cortisol) with high sensitivity for endogenous Cushing's. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Syndrome Guideline 2008)

options.text: Pituitary MRI with contrast as the initial test to identify a corticotroph adenoma before any biochemical confirmation of hypercortisolism
options.rationale: Pituitary MRI is performed only after biochemical confirmation of ACTH-dependent Cushing's syndrome; pituitary incidentalomas are common and imaging without biochemistry misleads. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Syndrome Guideline 2008)

options.text: Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling as the screening investigation to confirm pituitary versus ectopic ACTH source at first review today
options.rationale: Petrosal sinus sampling is reserved for distinguishing pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources after biochemical confirmation and ACTH-dependence, not as a screening test. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Syndrome Guideline 2008)

options.text: CT adrenals with contrast as an initial test to identify a primary adrenal tumour before any biochemical confirmation of cortisol excess present
options.rationale: Adrenal imaging is performed after biochemical confirmation; up to 5% of adults have incidentalomas, so imaging without biochemistry leads to overdiagnosis. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing's Syndrome Guideline 2008)

explanation.summary: The Endocrine Society recommends one of three first-line screening tests for Cushing's syndrome: overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test, 24-hour urinary free cortisol (two collections), or late-night salivary cortisol (two samples). Two abnormal first-line tests are required before further workup. Imaging is performed only after biochemical confirmation to avoid being misled by incidentalomas. Following confirmation, ACTH level dichotomises ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic) from ACTH-independent (adrenal) causes.
key_points: Three first-line screens: 1 mg DST, 24h UFC, late-night salivary cortisol
Two abnormal screens before further workup
Imaging only after biochemical confirmation
ACTH level dichotomises dependent versus independent disease
explanation.pearls: When a patient presents with new hypertension, central weight gain and wide violaceous striae, screen with overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression (cortisol <50 nmol/L next morning excludes Cushing's) and confirm with a second first-line test before imaging.

stem: A 68-year-old man is brought to your emergency department by ambulance with fever, rigors and confusion. He has urinary frequency for three days and known benign prostatic hyperplasia. After 30 mL/kg of crystalloid over the first hour, his blood pressure remains 84/48 mmHg with MAP 60 mmHg and his lactate has risen from 3.6 to 4.2 mmol/L. Blood cultures and a urine culture have been collected and broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics have been administered. There is no current contraindication to central venous access and central venous access can be obtained within 30 minutes.

data_table.Examination: Cool peripheries; capillary refill 4 seconds; mildly confused; tender suprapubic area
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 (was 3.6); WCC 18.2; Cr 142 (baseline 90); urine cloudy, leucocytes 3+

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next vasoactive therapy?

options.text: Start intravenous dopamine infusion at 5 micrograms per kilogram per minute as first-line vasoactive therapy via reliable peripheral access today
options.rationale: Dopamine has worse outcomes including more arrhythmias than noradrenaline in septic shock and is not recommended as first-line vasopressor. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer further 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over the next hour as primary therapy before initiating any vasopressor infusion today
options.rationale: Beyond the initial 30 mL/kg, further fluid in vasopressor-resistant septic shock risks pulmonary oedema; vasopressor initiation is preferred to maintain MAP. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence intravenous noradrenaline infusion via reliable access targeting mean arterial pressure 65 mmHg and initiate central access
options.rationale: Surviving Sepsis recommends commencing noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor after initial fluid resuscitation when MAP remains below 65 mmHg, including via reliable peripheral access while central access is obtained. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer intravenous metaraminol boluses to titrate blood pressure rather than initiating any continuous vasopressor infusion at this time today
options.rationale: Bolus metaraminol may temporise during set-up but is not a substitute for noradrenaline infusion in established fluid-refractory septic shock requiring sustained perfusion support. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Withhold vasopressor and re-resuscitate with 1 litre of 4% albumin instead of further crystalloid before reassessment of his perfusion status
options.rationale: Albumin may be used as an adjunct in patients receiving large-volume crystalloid resuscitation, but withholding vasopressor in fluid-refractory septic shock delays haemodynamic stabilisation. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: In septic shock with MAP below 65 mmHg despite an initial 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor. It can be commenced via a reliable peripheral large-bore line for short durations until central venous access is obtained. Vasopressin may be added when escalating noradrenaline doses are required. Beyond the initial bolus, further fluid is guided by dynamic measures rather than empiric repeat boluses to avoid pulmonary oedema.
key_points: Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor in septic shock
Target MAP is 65 mmHg
Vasopressor may be initiated peripherally while central access is sought
Dopamine has worse outcomes than noradrenaline
explanation.pearls: If MAP remains under 65 mmHg after the initial 30 mL/kg crystalloid in septic shock, commence noradrenaline via a reliable large-bore peripheral line while obtaining central access rather than giving further empirical fluid boluses.

stem: A 62-year-old woman is referred to your general medical clinic after incidental hypercalcaemia on a routine panel. She is asymptomatic but admits to mild constipation. She has osteopenia on a recent bone density scan. She takes no thiazide diuretics, lithium or calcium supplements and does not smoke. Family history includes her sister having a renal stone in her thirties. On examination she is well, with no lymphadenopathy and a normal respiratory and abdominal examination. Repeat fasting bloods on a separate day confirm the abnormality. The result pattern points to a single biochemical category before further imaging.

data_table.Examination: Well; no lymphadenopathy; abdomen soft non-tender; no bony tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 2.85 (RR 2.10-2.55) mmol/L; PTH 8.6 (RR 1.5-6.5) pmol/L; phosphate 0.74; 25-OH vit D 62; Cr 78; 24h urine Ca/Cr ratio 0.018
data_table.Medications: Cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily; aspirin 100 mg daily

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this biochemical pattern?

options.text: Vitamin D toxicity from prolonged supplementation with appropriately suppressed PTH and hyperphosphataemia from gut calcium absorption excess and bone resorption
options.rationale: Vitamin D toxicity suppresses PTH and is more commonly associated with elevated rather than low-normal phosphate; her PTH is elevated, ruling this out. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology 2024)

options.text: Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia given inherited calcium-sensing receptor mutation with similar PTH elevation and benign clinical course over years
options.rationale: FHH typically shows a urinary calcium-to-creatinine clearance ratio below 0.01; this patient's ratio of 0.018 excludes FHH on biochemistry. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology 2024)

options.text: Humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy from PTH-related peptide secretion with suppressed PTH driving phosphate wasting in the renal tubule
options.rationale: Malignancy-associated hypercalcaemia typically suppresses PTH; her PTH is elevated, which excludes humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology 2024)

options.text: Primary hyperparathyroidism with PTH-driven hypercalcaemia and normocalciuric phosphate-lowering profile consistent with autonomous parathyroid adenoma typically
options.rationale: Elevated calcium with non-suppressed PTH is the diagnostic pattern of primary hyperparathyroidism; the urinary calcium-to-creatinine ratio above 0.01 excludes familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology 2024)

options.text: Sarcoidosis driving 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity in granulomas with suppressed PTH and elevated 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D in a patient with subclinical disease
options.rationale: Granulomatous hypercalcaemia produces a PTH-suppressed pattern; her elevated PTH and absence of granulomatous features make this implausible. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology 2024)

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemia is divided into PTH-dependent (elevated or non-suppressed PTH) and PTH-independent (suppressed PTH) causes. PTH-dependent causes are primary or tertiary hyperparathyroidism and familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia, separated by the 24-hour urinary calcium-to-creatinine clearance ratio (less than 0.01 in FHH). PTH-independent causes include malignancy (PTH-rP, lytic lesions, calcitriol-secreting lymphoma), granulomatous disease, vitamin D or A toxicity, milk-alkali syndrome and immobility. Confirmation of primary hyperparathyroidism warrants imaging only when surgery is contemplated.
key_points: Elevated calcium with non-suppressed PTH = PTH-dependent
FHH separated by calcium-to-creatinine clearance ratio <0.01
Malignancy and granulomatous causes suppress PTH
Imaging only when surgery is contemplated
explanation.pearls: When hypercalcaemia presents with PTH above mid-normal and calcium-to-creatinine clearance ratio above 0.01, diagnose primary hyperparathyroidism without imaging first, then refer for parathyroid scintigraphy or 4D-CT only when surgical management is indicated.

stem:
A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to your emergency department with severe pain in his left lower leg that began yesterday after a minor garden scratch. The pain is dramatically out of proportion to the modest erythema seen on examination. Over six hours in the department the erythema has crept upward and a single haemorrhagic bulla has formed.

He is febrile and tachycardic. His bloods show neutrophil leucocytosis, sodium 130 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 78), glucose 14 mmol/L, CRP 320 mg/L and lactate 3.2 mmol/L. Wound swab is pending.

data_table.Examination: Severe pain disproportionate to redness; haemorrhagic bulla; cool peripheries
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22; Na 130; Cr 142 (baseline 78); glucose 14; CRP 320; Hb 132; lactate 3.2; LRINEC 9
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1000 mg BD; perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen referral as first-line therapy in suspected necrotising soft tissue infection with planning of operative debridement only afterwards
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is at most an adjunct after surgical debridement and broad antibiotic therapy in selected centres; it does not replace urgent operative source control. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g six-hourly for presumed simple cellulitis and reassess clinical progression of the affected limb in 12 hours
options.rationale: Simple cellulitis cover does not address gas-forming organisms or polymicrobial fascial infection, and reassessment delay in suspected necrotising fasciitis costs lives; surgery within 12 hours of presentation reduces mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024

options.text: MRI of the lower limb to confirm fascial involvement before any surgical consultation or empirical antibiotic escalation initiated in the department
options.rationale: Imaging delays definitive surgical exploration; necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis and surgery should not be delayed for MRI when the clinical and LRINEC suspicion is high. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin alone for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome cover without immediate surgical consultation or any antibiotic escalation today
options.rationale: IVIG is an adjunct in confirmed streptococcal toxic shock syndrome, not a substitute for source control or appropriate antibiotic combination; delay in surgery is the dominant predictor of mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024

options.text: Urgent surgical consultation for operative debridement combined with intravenous meropenem plus clindamycin while resuscitating from sepsis
options.rationale: LRINEC of 9 plus pain disproportionate to erythema, haemorrhagic bullae, rapid progression and sepsis strongly suggests necrotising fasciitis; urgent surgical exploration is the only intervention that reduces mortality, with broad-spectrum cover plus clindamycin for toxin suppression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infection requires urgent operative source control and broad-spectrum antibiotic cover with anti-toxin clindamycin. The LRINEC score (CRP, WCC, Hb, Na, Cr, glucose) above 6 raises and above 8 strongly raises suspicion, but no score replaces clinical assessment. Pain disproportionate to skin findings, rapid progression, haemorrhagic bullae, crepitus and systemic toxicity are red flags. Mortality rises sharply with each hour of surgical delay beyond 12 hours of presentation.
key_points: Necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis; do not delay surgery for imaging
LRINEC over 6 raises and over 8 strongly raises suspicion
Empirical cover includes broad-spectrum antibiotic plus clindamycin for toxin suppression
Mortality rises with each hour of operative delay beyond 12 hours
explanation.pearls: When LRINEC is 8 or higher with pain disproportionate to erythema and haemorrhagic bullae, call surgery within minutes and start meropenem plus clindamycin rather than waiting for MRI or persisting with simple cellulitis cover.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic: Skin and Soft Tissue Infections 2024

stem: A 31-year-old man presents to your sexual health clinic with a one-week history of fever, sore throat, generalised lymphadenopathy and a faint truncal rash. He reports a high-risk condomless receptive anal intercourse exposure approximately 18 days ago with a partner of unknown serostatus. He took no post-exposure prophylaxis. His fourth-generation HIV combination antigen and antibody test today is negative. He has no other infections identified on EBV serology, throat swab or chest imaging. He asks how confident you are in excluding HIV based on the current result alone today.

data_table.Examination: Cervical lymphadenopathy; mild pharyngeal erythema; truncal maculopapular rash
data_table.Investigations: 4th-gen Ag/Ab HIV: negative; EBV IgM neg; monospot neg; CRP 56

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: HIV-1 RNA viral load by NAAT now to detect acute seroconversion within the eclipse and window phase before antibody seroconversion completes
options.rationale: Fourth-generation Ag/Ab assays can be negative during early viraemia before p24 antigenaemia rises; HIV RNA NAAT detects infection within around 10 days of exposure and is the test of choice when acute seroconversion is suspected and Ag/Ab is negative. (Source: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy)
source_refs: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy

options.text: Repeat 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination test in three months to definitively exclude infection without any further testing required today
options.rationale: A three-month repeat alone misses the acute seroconversion window now and delays diagnosis of likely current acute HIV infection in this high-risk symptomatic presentation. (Source: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy)
source_refs: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy

options.text: Western blot HIV antibody confirmatory test to override the screening negative and detect early seroconverting antibody bands before clinical resolution
options.rationale: Western blot is a confirmatory antibody test and is also negative in early seroconversion before antibody develops; HIV RNA NAAT is the appropriate sensitive test in this window. (Source: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy)
source_refs: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy

options.text: Commence empirical antiretroviral therapy now without further testing given the high pretest probability of acute HIV in this clinical presentation today
options.rationale: Empirical antiretroviral therapy without diagnostic confirmation is not standard of care; HIV RNA NAAT provides diagnostic confirmation rapidly to guide treatment initiation. (Source: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy)
source_refs: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy

options.text: CD4 cell count immediately to identify acute HIV-related lymphopenia as evidence of active infection before any further screening or molecular testing
options.rationale: CD4 count drops transiently in acute HIV but is non-specific and is used to stage established infection rather than diagnose acute infection in the eclipse-window phase. (Source: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy)
source_refs: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents 2 to 4 weeks after exposure with a mononucleosis-like illness. Fourth-generation antigen and antibody combination assays narrow but do not eliminate the diagnostic window because p24 antigenaemia can be missed in very early disease. HIV RNA NAAT detects viraemia within around 10 days of exposure and is the recommended test when acute infection is suspected and the fourth-generation result is negative. Early identification matters for prompt initiation of antiretroviral therapy and contact tracing.
key_points: Acute HIV mimics mononucleosis 2 to 4 weeks post-exposure
4th-gen Ag/Ab narrows but does not eliminate the diagnostic window
HIV RNA NAAT detects viraemia from around 10 days post-exposure
Early diagnosis enables immediate ART and contact tracing
explanation.pearls: When a sexually exposed patient presents within four weeks of risk with a mononucleosis-like illness and negative 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab, order HIV RNA NAAT immediately rather than waiting three months to repeat antibody testing.

sources.label: ASHM Australian National HIV Testing Policy

stem: A 78-year-old man on apixaban 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation presents to your emergency department six hours after a witnessed unwitnessed fall at home. CT brain demonstrates a 12 mm right frontal acute subdural haematoma with 4 mm of midline shift. His last apixaban dose was four hours before his fall and his renal function is normal. The neurosurgical registrar advises operative evacuation in the next two hours. His weight is 78 kg, he has no history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, and there are no contraindications to specific factor Xa inhibitor reversal.

data_table.Examination: Mild left arm weakness 4/5; PEARL; no other focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122; platelets 188; INR 1.2; Cr 78 (eGFR 78); CT: 12 mm SDH, 4 mm midline shift
data_table.Medications: Apixaban 5 mg BD; perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: Which reversal strategy is most appropriate before surgery?

options.text: Administer 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 50 units per kilogram intravenously as the only available reversal in many Australian hospital sites
options.rationale: PCC is a reasonable option where andexanet alfa is unavailable, but where andexanet alfa is accessible it is the specific direct reversal agent for apixaban with stronger evidence for haemostatic efficacy in intracranial haemorrhage. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023

options.text: Administer andexanet alfa via the high-dose intravenous bolus and infusion regimen because last apixaban dose was within eight hours of intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: Andexanet alfa is the specific reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors and is recommended for life-threatening or intracranial bleeding when last dose was within hours; the high-dose regimen applies to apixaban over 5 mg or last dose under eight hours ago. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023

options.text: Administer fresh frozen plasma 15 mL per kilogram intravenously to dilute circulating apixaban and provide some non-specific factor replacement support
options.rationale: FFP does not reverse direct factor Xa inhibitors and risks volume overload without providing meaningful haemostatic benefit in DOAC-associated bleeding. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023

options.text: Administer intravenous vitamin K 10 mg slowly with platelet transfusion to enhance clotting in the face of an ongoing acute intracranial haemorrhage now
options.rationale: Vitamin K does not reverse direct factor Xa inhibitors and platelet transfusion has no role unless the platelet count is critically low; neither addresses the underlying anticoagulant effect. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023

options.text: Administer idarucizumab 5 g intravenously as a single dose to reverse direct anticoagulant activity prior to neurosurgical intervention proceeding
options.rationale: Idarucizumab is the specific reversal agent for dabigatran, not apixaban; using it would have no reversal effect on factor Xa inhibitor activity. (Source: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023)
source_refs: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023

explanation.summary: Life-threatening bleeding on apixaban or rivaroxaban is reversed with andexanet alfa where available; the high-dose regimen (800 mg bolus and 8 mg per minute infusion) applies when the apixaban dose was more than 5 mg or taken less than 8 hours before the event. Where andexanet alfa is unavailable, 4-factor PCC at 50 units per kilogram is the accepted alternative. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran only. FFP, vitamin K and platelet transfusion are not appropriate substitutes for specific reversal in DOAC-associated bleeding.
key_points: Andexanet alfa specifically reverses factor Xa inhibitor activity
High-dose andexanet for apixaban over 5 mg or last dose under 8 hours ago
Idarucizumab is for dabigatran, not apixaban
4-factor PCC 50 units per kilogram is the alternative when andexanet unavailable
explanation.pearls: For apixaban-associated intracranial haemorrhage with last dose under eight hours ago, give andexanet alfa high-dose (800 mg bolus then 8 mg per minute infusion) rather than reaching for FFP, vitamin K or idarucizumab which provide no factor Xa inhibitor reversal.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand Society of Blood Transfusion DOAC Reversal Statement 2023

stem:
A 41-year-old man is referred to your endocrine inpatient team with episodic severe headache, palpitations and diaphoresis over six months, now with sustained hypertension. He has been resistant to standard agents. He is awake, alert and currently haemodynamically stable. Plasma metanephrines are markedly elevated and CT abdomen shows a 4.5 cm right adrenal mass with classic imaging features. The surgical registrar suggests starting metoprolol now for the tachycardia.

He has no current angina, no asthma, and good background renal function. He is reviewed today for medical preparation prior to laparoscopic adrenalectomy in three weeks.

data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic; otherwise well; no orthostasis on standing today
data_table.Investigations: Plasma metanephrines markedly elevated; CT 4.5 cm right adrenal mass with washout pattern; eGFR 88
data_table.Medications: Amlodipine 5 mg daily; perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate medical preparation strategy now?

options.text: Commence amlodipine dose escalation and add an angiotensin receptor blocker alone without any specific catecholamine-targeted pharmacological preparation prior to surgery
options.rationale: Calcium channel and renin-angiotensin blockers are adjuncts only; they do not provide adequate intraoperative haemodynamic protection without alpha-blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline 2014)

options.text: Commence metoprolol now to control the tachycardia and add prazosin in two weeks once heart rate is consistently below 80 beats per minute on review
options.rationale: Initiating beta-blockade before adequate alpha-blockade in phaeochromocytoma can precipitate severe hypertensive crisis through unopposed alpha vasoconstriction; this sequence is contraindicated. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline 2014)

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine alpha-blockade titrated to mild orthostatic hypotension; introduce beta-blockade only after adequate alpha-blockade is established
options.rationale: Unopposed alpha-receptor stimulation after beta-blockade alone precipitates hypertensive crisis in phaeochromocytoma; non-selective alpha-blockade with phenoxybenzamine for 10 to 14 days, titrated to mild orthostasis, must precede any beta-blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline 2014)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparoscopic adrenalectomy within 48 hours without any specific preoperative pharmacological preparation in a young patient at lower operative risk
options.rationale: Unprepared phaeochromocytoma surgery carries unacceptable risk of intraoperative hypertensive crisis, arrhythmia and death; 10 to 14 days of alpha-blockade is standard preparation. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline 2014)

options.text: Commence labetalol monotherapy as a combined alpha- and beta-blocker for preoperative preparation given its dual receptor activity simplifying medication titration logistics
options.rationale: Labetalol has a beta-blocking effect that exceeds its alpha-blocking effect and is not recommended as monotherapy preparation for phaeochromocytoma surgery. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline 2014)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma requires careful preoperative medical preparation to prevent intraoperative haemodynamic catastrophe. Non-selective alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) or selective alpha-blockade (doxazosin or prazosin) is started 10 to 14 days preoperatively and titrated to mild orthostatic hypotension. Beta-blockade is added only after adequate alpha-blockade because beta-blockade alone unmasks unopposed alpha-vasoconstriction. Salt and fluid loading expand the contracted intravascular volume in the days before surgery. Calcium channel blockers are useful adjuncts when blood pressure remains elevated.
key_points: Alpha-blockade always precedes beta-blockade in phaeochromocytoma
Phenoxybenzamine titrated to mild orthostasis for 10 to 14 days
Beta-blockade alone risks hypertensive crisis through unopposed alpha
Salt and fluid loading expand the contracted intravascular volume
explanation.pearls: When a confirmed phaeochromocytoma patient is scheduled for adrenalectomy in three weeks, start phenoxybenzamine and titrate to mild postural drop over 10 to 14 days before adding any beta-blocker, regardless of how prominent the tachycardia or anxiety symptoms appear at baseline.

stem: A 29-year-old woman gravida 2 para 1 at 39 weeks and 2 days gestation presents to your birth suite in spontaneous labour with regular painful contractions every 3 minutes and a cervix dilated to 5 cm. Her routine vaginal-rectal culture at 36 weeks was positive for group B Streptococcus. She has no allergies. Her membranes are still intact and the cardiotocograph is reassuring. She had an uncomplicated previous vaginal birth. She received no antenatal antibiotics. The midwife seeks your antibiotic order.

data_table.Examination: 5 cm dilated; membranes intact; vertex; CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: GBS LVS positive at 36 weeks; FBC and group and screen normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum chemoprophylaxis?

options.text: Intravenous ampicillin 2 g loading then 1 g four-hourly until delivery as the recommended first-line agent for intrapartum chemoprophylaxis in penicillin-tolerant women
options.rationale: Ampicillin is an acceptable alternative but the RANZCOG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommendation in Australia is benzylpenicillin as the first-line agent in penicillin-tolerant women. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2022)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g eight-hourly until delivery as a simpler regimen that avoids intravenous access in low-risk women with intact membranes still in labour
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin has not been shown to achieve adequate amniotic fluid concentrations for GBS prevention; intravenous administration is required. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2022)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 900 mg eight-hourly as primary chemoprophylaxis to cover possible undocumented penicillin allergy in a woman without recorded allergies today
options.rationale: Clindamycin is reserved for women with documented severe penicillin hypersensitivity and confirmed clindamycin susceptibility; it is not used routinely. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2022)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g four-hourly until delivery according to current recommended Australian intrapartum chemoprophylaxis dosing
options.rationale: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery is first-line intrapartum chemoprophylaxis in penicillin-tolerant women with GBS colonisation. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2022)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until membrane rupture occurs given intact membranes are protective and there is no current acute risk factor for early-onset neonatal sepsis today
options.rationale: Intrapartum chemoprophylaxis is given as soon as labour is established regardless of membrane status to allow adequate amniotic fluid concentrations before delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2022)

explanation.summary: Intrapartum antibiotic chemoprophylaxis for GBS-colonised women reduces early-onset neonatal GBS disease. First-line is intravenous benzylpenicillin (3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery) given as soon as labour is established or membranes rupture, irrespective of membrane status. Cefazolin is used for low-risk penicillin allergy. Clindamycin (with confirmed susceptibility) or vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy. Adequate prophylaxis requires at least 4 hours before delivery for optimal benefit.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g every 4 hours is first-line
Cefazolin for low-risk penicillin allergy
Clindamycin with susceptibility or vancomycin for severe allergy
Optimal benefit requires at least 4 hours before delivery
explanation.pearls: When a GBS-positive woman is in labour with a non-allergic history, give benzylpenicillin 3 g IV stat then 1.8 g every 4 hours; aim for at least one dose 4 hours before delivery to maximise neonatal protection rather than withholding until membrane rupture.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman attends your general practice clinic with a four-year history of irregular menstrual cycles of variable length up to 90 days and increasing facial hirsutism. She has gained 6 kg in two years and is not currently using contraception. She is not seeking pregnancy at present. Her examination shows BMI 28 with mild upper-lip and chin terminal hair.

Her bloods show normal prolactin, normal TSH, normal 17-hydroxyprogesterone and elevated free androgen index. Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates a single ovary with 14 follicles measuring 2 to 9 mm.

data_table.Examination: BMI 28; mild facial hirsutism; no acanthosis nigricans; no virilisation
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.6; prolactin 320; 17-OHP normal; FAI elevated; ovary 14 follicles 2-9 mm

lead_in: Does this presentation meet diagnostic criteria for polycystic ovary syndrome?

options.text: No; without prior progestogen-induced withdrawal bleed challenge testing oligo-ovulation cannot be confirmed and PCOS criteria cannot yet be applied to this clinical case
options.rationale: Cycle history of variable cycles to 90 days establishes oligo-ovulation; progestogen challenge is not required for the diagnosis. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023

options.text: No; PCOS requires the full triad of oligo-ovulation, hyperandrogenism and polycystic morphology together with exclusion of mimickers and at least one metabolic feature present
options.rationale: Rotterdam criteria require any two of the three features, not all three, after exclusion of mimics; metabolic features are not part of the diagnostic criteria. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023

options.text: No; her ovarian morphology threshold of 14 follicles is below the current diagnostic cut-off using newer transvaginal ultrasound technology with higher resolution imaging
options.rationale: The 2023 international guideline lowered the threshold for polycystic ovarian morphology to 20 or more follicles per ovary on high-frequency transducers, but the criterion is met by the combination of clinical, biochemical and morphological features in adults. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023

options.text: Yes; she meets PCOS criteria but only because AMH level above the laboratory cut-off has replaced ultrasound morphology in current diagnostic frameworks worldwide
options.rationale: AMH can substitute for morphology in adults under the 2023 guideline but ultrasound morphology is still valid; the diagnosis here rests on oligo-ovulation, hyperandrogenism and morphology. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023

options.text: Yes; she meets two of three Rotterdam criteria after exclusion of mimicking causes including thyroid, prolactin and adrenal cortical disorders biochemically today
options.rationale: Rotterdam criteria require two of three: oligo-ovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology after exclusion of mimics; this patient meets all three. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023

explanation.summary: PCOS in adults is diagnosed by Rotterdam criteria, with the 2023 international evidence-based guideline retaining two-of-three features (oligo-ovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology) after exclusion of mimickers such as thyroid disorders, hyperprolactinaemia, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, Cushing's syndrome and androgen-secreting tumours. Polycystic ovarian morphology threshold is 20 or more follicles per ovary on high-frequency transducers or AMH above the laboratory threshold. Initial management addresses cycle regulation, fertility planning and cardiometabolic risk.
key_points: Rotterdam two-of-three: oligo-ovulation, hyperandrogenism, morphology
Exclude thyroid, prolactin, CAH, Cushing's, androgen tumours
Morphology threshold is 20+ follicles per ovary on high-frequency transducer
AMH may substitute for ultrasound morphology in adults
explanation.pearls: When cycle length consistently exceeds 35 days plus hirsutism and elevated free androgen index occur with normal mimicker bloods, diagnose PCOS without further confirmatory testing rather than requiring all three Rotterdam features.

sources.label: International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS 2023
